verses.text: Hear, Israel: thou art to pass over Jordan this day to possess nations greater and mightier than thyself, cities fenced up to heaven, the children of the Anakims, of whom thou hast heard, Who can stand before the children of Anak! Understand this day, that the LORD thy God is He which goeth over before thee — as a consuming fire He shall destroy them. Guidance: Moses doesn't soften the threat. The cities are fenced up to heaven, the men are giants, the saying is famous. Then the pivot: 'understand THIS day' — the LORD goes ahead as fire. The truth that wins is not the size of the enemy, but the size of the One in front of you.

Speak not in thine heart: For my righteousness the LORD hath brought me to possess this land. Not for thy righteousness, or thine upright heart — but for the wickedness of these nations, and that He may perform the word He sware unto thy fathers. Thou art a stiffnecked people. Guidance: A blunt sentence designed to keep them honest at the moment of victory. The land is given because of the Canaanites' sin, the fathers' promise, and nothing you contributed. You are stiffnecked. Pride is more dangerous than the giants, because it cannot be killed by a sword.

Remember, forget not, how thou provokedst the LORD thy God to wrath in the wilderness: from the day thou didst depart out of Egypt unto this place, ye have been rebellious against the LORD. Also in Horeb ye provoked the LORD, so that the LORD was angry with you to have destroyed you. Guidance: Moses' way of fighting the coming arrogance is brutal honesty about the past. From Egypt to here, you have been rebellious. The whole walk was a record of mercy, not merit. Memory is the cure for self-righteousness — but only if it doesn't lie.

When I was gone up into the mount to receive the tables of stone, I abode forty days and nights, neither did eat bread nor drink water. The LORD said: get thee down quickly, for thy people have corrupted themselves; made them a molten image. Let Me alone, that I may destroy them; I will make of thee a nation mightier than they. Guidance: God said it out loud — let Me alone, I will make YOU a nation. The offer was real. Most leaders would have taken it. Moses didn't even hesitate to refuse. Sometimes a calling proves itself by what it declines.

I turned and came down from the mount, the mount burned with fire: the two tables of the covenant were in my two hands. I looked, behold, ye had sinned, made you a molten calf — quickly turned aside out of the way the LORD had commanded. I took the two tables, cast them out of my two hands, brake them before your eyes. Guidance: The stones go in his hands, the calf swirls in front of him, and Moses smashes the marriage contract on the threshold. A symbolic divorce — the covenant they just made was already broken in their hearts before he could put it in their hands.

I fell down before the LORD forty days and nights, neither bread nor water — for I was afraid of the LORD's wrath against you. But the LORD hearkened. He was very angry with Aaron to have destroyed him: I prayed for Aaron also. I burnt the calf, stamped it, ground it small as dust, cast it into the brook. Guidance: Two forty-day fasts back to back — Moses stood between the lightning and his people. He even prayed for Aaron, who had built the calf. And the calf? Ground to powder and drunk by the brook. Idols don't deserve a museum.

At Taberah, Massah, Kibroth-hattaavah, ye provoked the LORD to wrath. Likewise when the LORD sent you from Kadesh-barnea, saying, Go up and possess the land — then ye rebelled, ye believed Him not, nor hearkened. Ye have been rebellious against the LORD from the day I knew you. Guidance: Four place-names to remind them — places of fire, water-strife, graves-of-craving, and the great refusal. Moses isn't shaming; he is preparing. The next chapter is fragile because the next generation still has the same heart. Pride must die before the river.

I fell down before the LORD forty days and nights, because He said He would destroy you. I prayed: O Lord GOD, destroy not Thy people, which Thou hast redeemed through Thy greatness, brought out of Egypt. Remember Thy servants, Abraham, Isaac, Jacob; look not unto the stubbornness of this people. Lest the land whence Thou broughtest us out say, the LORD was not able. Yet they are Thy people, which Thou broughtest out by Thy mighty power. Guidance: The greatest intercession in the Torah. Moses pleads on three grounds: your investment (redeemed at great cost), your promise (Abraham), and your reputation (lest Egypt mock). When you have no merit to plead, plead God's own concerns. He listens.
